title: Aster glutinosus
taxonomy: Aster glutinosus belongs to the family Asteraceae, commonly known as the daisy or sunflower family. This family is a large and diverse group of flowering plants that includes over 23,000 species. Aster glutinosus is further classified within the genus Aster, which consists of annual and perennial plants. The genus Aster is part of the tribe Astereae, which is a subfamily within the Asteraceae family. The tribe Astereae is characterized by its composite flowers, which are made up of multiple smaller flowers.
uses: Aster glutinosus has been used in traditional medicine for various purposes. It is believed to have anti-inflammatory and antiseptic properties, which can be beneficial in treating wounds and skin conditions. In some cultures, the plant is used to treat respiratory issues such as bronchitis and coughs. Its leaves and flowers are often used in teas and infusions to promote relaxation and reduce stress. Additionally, the plant is said to have antimicrobial properties, which can help prevent the growth of bacteria and other microorganisms.

intro: Aster glutinosus, also known as sticky aster or sweet white aster, is a perennial herbaceous plant belonging to the daisy family (Asteraceae). The plant has been used in traditional medicine for its health benefits, including its anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antiseptic properties, which can help to soothe wounds, reduce fever, and alleviate respiratory issues. The bioactive constituents of Aster glutinosus include essential oils, flavonoids, and sesquiterpenoids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In traditional herbal medicine, Aster glutinosus is often used to make teas, infusions, and tinctures, as well as being used in poultices and salves to aid in the treatment of skin conditions and wounds.
